title: W6: Post-Training with Reinforcement Learning
blurb: Highest priority for post-training. RLHF, reward models, KL regularization, GRPO, and inference-time compute are all represented in recent exams.

knowledge_points.title: RLHF and Reward Models
knowledge_points.recap: RLHF pipeline: SFT model, collect human preference comparisons, train a reward model, optimize policy against the reward model with KL regularization.

questions.q:
LLMs are pretrained to predict the next token. To allow them to follow instructions, they must be finetuned.

1. Explain the three main steps for finetuning an LLM with reinforcement learning with human feedback. No equations required. (3 points)
questions.answer:
**(a) Supervised fine-tuning** of a pretrained LLM on high-quality data for the desired downstream task **(1 point)**. Supervised fine-tuning must be mentioned.

**(b) Collect comparison data from human feedback and train a reward model (1 point).** Training a reward model on data collected from humans must be made clear.

**(c) Optimize a policy against the reward model using reinforcement learning (1 point).** RL or PPO must be mentioned.
questions.extend: Memory: RLHF pipeline = SFT -> human preference reward model -> RL optimize policy.

questions.q: 2. For Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) we need a reward model. What are the inputs and labels used to train the reward model, and why do we need a reward model at all for preference alignment? (3 points)
questions.answer: The reward model is trained on **pairs of responses generated by the supervised fine-tuned model for the same prompt**, i.e. the input is **a prompt together with two candidate responses $(x,y_1,y_2)$ (1 point)**. The label is **a human preference indicating which response is better, e.g. $y_1\succ y_2$ (1 point)**. We need a reward model because during RL training the model generates many responses that need to be evaluated, and **having humans evaluate every single response would be too expensive and does not scale (1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: reward model turns human pairwise preferences into scalable automatic rewards for RL.

questions.q:
The RLHF objective contains a reward term and a KL divergence term to a reference model.

2. What is the purpose of the KL divergence term in the RLHF objective? (1 point)
questions.answer: The KL divergence term is included to **regularize the learned policy**, ensuring it does not deviate too much from the reference model $\pi_{ref}$. This helps prevent issues such as **reward hacking**, since the reference model, typically a supervised fine-tuned model, already generates coherent text **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: KL keeps the RL policy close to the SFT/reference model.

questions.q:
Computing the policy gradient for RLHF. For

$$L(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{x\sim D,y\sim\pi_\theta}\left[r(x,y)-\beta(\log\pi_\theta(y|x)-\log\pi_{ref}(y|x))\right],$$

show using the log-derivative trick why the gradient contains

$$r(x,y)-\beta(1+\log\pi_\theta(y|x)-\log\pi_{ref}(y|x)).$$
questions.answer:
Let $p_	heta(x,y)=D(x)\pi_	heta(y|x)$ and $f_	heta=r- eta(\log\pi_	heta-\log\pi_{ref})$. By the product rule,

$$
abla(p_	heta f_	heta)=(
abla p_	heta)f_	heta+p_	heta
abla f_	heta.$$

The log-derivative trick gives **$
abla p_	heta=p_	heta
abla\log\pi_	heta$**. Also **$
abla f_	heta=- eta
abla\log\pi_	heta$**. Factoring $
abla\log\pi_	heta$ gives the extra **$1$** in the KL term.
questions.extend: Memory: the extra 1 comes from differentiating the explicit $\log\pi_\theta$ term.

questions.q:
Reducing policy-gradient variance. For

$$g=\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(y|x)(r(x,y)-b(x)),$$

show the variance-minimizing baseline and explain when it becomes the average reward.
questions.answer:
Minimize

$$\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(y|x)\|^2(r(x,y)-b(x))^2].$$

Differentiating with respect to $b(x)$ gives

$$b^*=\frac{\mathbb{E}[r(x,y)\|\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(y|x)\|^2]}{\mathbb{E}[\|\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(y|x)\|^2]}.$$

If the gradient norm is approximately constant, it cancels, so **$b^*\approx\mathbb{E}[r(x,y)]$**.
questions.extend: Memory: optimal baseline is a gradient-norm weighted reward average.

questions.q:
Tutorial 7: RLHF. What is the time complexity of computing

$$J(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{y\sim\pi_\theta(\cdot|x)}[r(x,y)]$$

for sequences of length $T$ if only the final output has reward? How does this change with token-level rewards?
questions.answer:
With only final reward, exact computation sums over all length-$T$ sequences, so complexity is **$O(|V|^T)$**.

With token-level rewards, one sums over all prefixes at each time step:

$$\sum_{t=1}^T |V|^t=O(|V|^{T+1}).$$

This is still exponential, so realistic RLHF uses **Monte Carlo estimators such as REINFORCE**.
questions.extend: Memory: exact text RL expectations are exponentially large.

questions.q: Tutorial 7: RLHF. Let $\pi_\theta(a)=N(\theta,1)$ and $r(a)=a+C$. The score function is $\nabla_\theta\log\pi_\theta(a)=a-\theta$, so $g=(a-\theta)(a+C)$. With $a=\theta+x$, $x\sim N(0,1)$, derive $\operatorname{Var}[g]$ and the minimizing $C^*$.
questions.answer:
With $a=\theta+x$,

$$g=x(\theta+x+C)=x^2+x(\theta+C).$$

Using $E[x^2]=1$, $E[x^3]=0$, $E[x^4]=3$, we get **$E[g]=1$** and

$$E[g^2]=3+(\theta+C)^2.$$

Thus

$$\operatorname{Var}[g]=2+(\theta+C)^2.$$

This is minimized by **$C^*=-\theta$**.
questions.extend: Memory: reward shifts can strongly change REINFORCE variance.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): In RLHF, why is a KL penalty to the reference policy often added during policy optimization?
questions.answer: The KL penalty discourages the updated policy from moving too far from the reference/SFT policy. This helps prevent reward hacking, language-quality collapse, and overly large updates that exploit imperfections in the reward model. It trades off reward maximization against staying close to a known-good policy.
questions.extend: Memory: KL keeps RL optimization anchored to the SFT model.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why can optimizing a learned reward model produce behavior that humans do not actually prefer?
questions.answer: The reward model is only an imperfect proxy for human preferences. The policy may find outputs that score highly according to reward-model artifacts but are unhelpful, verbose, unsafe, or misleading. This is a form of **reward hacking** or over-optimization of the proxy.
questions.extend: Memory: reward model score is not identical to true human preference.

knowledge_points.title: GRPO and Inference-Time Compute
knowledge_points.recap: Reasoning RL often uses group-relative advantages. Inference-time compute can also improve answers without changing weights.

questions.q:
Learning to Reason. In GRPO, for each prompt $x$, $G$ completions $\{y_i\}_{i=1}^G$ are generated by sampling from the old policy and each completion receives a reward.

1. Suppose for one prompt $x$, GRPO samples $G=8$ completions where seven receive reward $r_i=1$ and one receives reward $r_i=0$. Compute the normalized advantages $\hat A_i$ and describe the resulting policy-gradient contribution of this prompt. (2 points)
questions.answer:
The group mean reward is

$$\mu=\frac{7\cdot1+1\cdot0}{8}=\frac78.$$

The standard deviation is

$$\sigma=\sqrt{\frac{7(1-7/8)^2+1(0-7/8)^2}{8}}=\frac{\sqrt7}{8}.$$

**Mean and standard deviation correct: 0.5 points.** The normalized advantages are **(0.5 points)**:

For the seven completions with $r_i=1$:

$$\hat A_i=\frac{1-7/8}{\sqrt7/8}=\frac{1}{\sqrt7}>0.$$

For the one completion with $r_i=0$:

$$\hat A_i=\frac{0-7/8}{\sqrt7/8}=-\sqrt7<0.$$

The policy is updated to **increase the probability of completions with $r_i=1$ (0.5 points)** and **decrease the probability of the completion with $r_i=0$ (0.5 points)**.
questions.extend: Memory: GRPO normalizes within the group. Positive advantage increases probability; negative advantage decreases it.

questions.q: Learning to Reason. 2. You take a pre-trained base model and perform RL for reasoning with GRPO. At the beginning of training, every completion receives zero reward. Does training progress? If not, how can you modify your training pipeline to benefit from RL fine-tuning? (2 points)
questions.answer:
**No, training does not progress (1 point).** If every completion receives zero reward, $r_i=0$ for all $i$, so the group-normalized advantage $\hat A_i=0$ for all $i$. The gradient is zero and the policy does not update.

To benefit from RL fine-tuning, **first perform supervised fine-tuning on the base model** so it becomes better at the task, and then do RL fine-tuning with the supervised fine-tuned model as the base model **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: all rewards equal -> all normalized advantages zero -> no policy gradient signal.

questions.q: 4. Explain one approach discussed in class for improving the performance of a language model by increasing inference-time compute. (2 points)
questions.answer:
Any of the following gives credit for **naming the approach (1 point)** and **explaining it correctly (1 point)**:

**Majority voting:** run the model multiple times on the same prompt and pick the most common answer. This works well when there is one correct output, e.g. a number in math problems.

**Search-based methods:** let the model generate partial solutions and use a search algorithm to explore different reasoning paths and select the best one, e.g. Tree of Thoughts.

**Chain-of-thought:** train the model to generate reasoning traces; using CoT increases inference-time compute because response length increases. Only mentioning fine-tuning does not give points, because the question asks about more resources during inference, not training.
questions.extend: Memory: inference-time compute means spend more tokens/samples/search at test time, not more training.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): What is the intuition behind using group-relative advantages in GRPO-style training?
questions.answer: Instead of needing a separate value model, multiple completions for the same prompt can be compared against their group average. A completion that scores above the group mean gets positive advantage, and one below the group mean gets negative advantage. This gives a relative learning signal for the prompt.
questions.extend: Memory: GRPO compares samples within the same prompt group.

questions.q: Supplemental practice (not a direct exam question): Why might inference-time compute improve reasoning performance even without changing model weights?
questions.answer: Extra inference-time compute can sample multiple candidate solutions, allow longer reasoning traces, or use verification/reranking. Even if the model weights stay fixed, searching over more attempts can increase the chance of finding a correct solution, especially for sparse-reward reasoning tasks.
questions.extend: Memory: inference-time scaling uses more search or samples, not more training.
